rhubarb: To be honest, I think you resigned too soon. There's a lot of the board still to play on--even if you make a mistake in one part, you can still learn from trying your best on the other parts of the board :)

C[rhubarb: hey fwiw I think you're making some really good decisions this game. I'll make a couple comments on past moves.
rhubarb: (whoops, typing in all the wrong boxes.) Generally, there are two standard options in a crosscut situation: extend or atari. You chose atari on the E5 stone. Not a bad choice!
rhubarb: Here's one ""extend"" option. Also fine.
rhubarb: Here's a rule of thumb, though: extend if you want to live on both sides of the crosscut
column2: atari if you want to sacrifice one side.

column1: rhubarb: So when you atari here, you might want to think about sacrificing the E4 stone in exchange for something elsewhere (maybe pushing white around while making life above).

rhubarb: Up to here, it looks like you're playing consistently with that plan from the crosscut: you're getting stronger above, but as you push me, your stones at E4 and C3 are getting weaker. You might try giving up the E4 stone to save C3, or vice versa (maybe use G3 and E4 to strengthen each other)
rhubarb: OK, protecting against the cut. This is a sensible thing to think about. Your stones on the top are now very ""thick"" (hard to cut). My F4-G4 group is looking weak.
rhubarb: Tenuki! (Playing elsewhere, ignoring my move.) This is great--you don't want to let the other player push you around
column2: seeing more important moves elsewhere on the board is a sign of maturity. My F4-G4 group is EXTRA unhappy now. O

column1: rhubarb: (are these comments getting associated with the right moves or just showing up in the chat live, so to speak? I'll move them around if so.)

rhubarb: This move at H5 is unnecessary, though. I wasn't going to be able to cut there if I tried.

rhubarb: Yes! This is what I was trying to get you to play when I played at C7 way back when--you ignored me, did some other stuff you wanted to do, and then came back here before I had a chance to follow up with a second move in the area. Your tenuki got you this move/that sequence for free.

C[rhubarb: Playing in front of black's shimari is a good idea, but this is getting too close to a strong position. Better to play nearer the bottom side star point.

C[rhubarb: I think this is a mistake for black. Better to approach the bottom left corner, in part to help out the relatively weak M3/M5 stones--they're only attacking white's O3 group if they can stay alive!

C[rhubarb: I'm not sure this is a good move for black, but I stink at joseki, so who knows? Extending back along the top side looks like a better idea to me now, or (again) buttressing the M3/M5 stones.
C[rhubarb: This seems like the right idea--attack the M3 stones to save the O3 group.

C[rhubarb: ...but this doesn't look inconsistent with the strategy behind the last few white moves. The idea was to save the O3 group by attacking the black group to the left, not by making eyes in a small space, or by attacking the relatively strong black stones in the corner. Better to play now something like wN5-bM6-wM4, I think, to try & break through to the left.

C[rhubarb: I think white should not ignore this. If the M3 group connects to the corner on the first line, it'll live, and white's O3 group will die. Even if white has given up on the O3 group, better to attack the black M3 group from the left to force black to capture the O3 group & build up white thickness on the outside.
C[rhubarb: Nice--black shouldn't have left that weak M17 stone all alone!
C[rhubarb: Better to try the cut at L16/M16, I think.

C[rhubarb: Maybe better to try the cut at E17, since the ladder doesn't work. As we'll see, aiming to capture the H17 stone without strengthening the corner will allow black to take the whole corner--which is more than worth the two stones black sacrifices in the process.

C[rhubarb: Definitely better to play in the corner here. Even if black's H17/G16 stones manage to live, white's top side group should be able to escape into the centre, at least. Plus, there's still the chance of connecting to white's top right group. All this is to say that there's nothing urgent to demand blocking black to the right.

C[rhubarb: As we'll see, better to connect at F15--black's playing here to set up the cut at F14. Ignoring black F19 isn't going to result in a loss of more than a handful of points--not big at this stage of the game.

C[rhubarb: Because D4 is high, white doesn't have the corner--so this looks like a move to defend something white doesn't have yet. Better to play something on the third line on the left side (white's left side moyo is quite open, and black has thickness facing into it from above). Even D3 would work better--as well as blocking off the corner from this direction, that puts some pressure on black's group to the right.

C[rhubarb: This is questionable. Black is forcing white to strengthen the connection to the C2 stone--which white wants to do. I guess black was aiming for some reduction of eyespace in the corner, but I still think it might be better to go straight for the cut at E5.

C[rhubarb: Black is aiming to cut white's corner off. I don't think white can avoid having the corner cut off from one of two directions (above or to the right), but if the corner gets cut off from *both* directions, it'll have trouble surviving--and plus, black C6 will probably then wind up connected to the lower side group. White needs to choose which direction to save.
C[rhubarb: C5 instead. Black can either capture C6 or connect at D6, but not both. Either way, white has a good chance of saving the corner.
C[rhubarb: I think A2 instead of B1 makes life for the corner.
C[rhubarb: From here, black starts exploiting the thickness he's built up to break white's frameworks.

C[rhubarb: I think this was a mistake. Both white groups had four liberties before this move, but I think the big one is more vulnerable. Black can more easily hane at B4 than at J5, so it would have been better to play somewhere on the left.
rhubarb: Oh, or it would make sense for white to start attacking the weak black group in the corner. A play at J4 would both help white's top-right group and attack black.
rhubarb: Just to finish that last thought: after white J4, black can't hane at B4 for fear of wJ3, bJ2, wJ5, and then if white can get H1 black dies in a snapback.
Therefore, I think J4 is the best play for white in this situation.
